steps.title: Identify the claim to support
steps.content: The question asks for the quotation that best supports the claim that the professor cannot adjust to life at the estate. The evidence must come from the professor's own words expressing explicit discomfort or rejection of estate life.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice by rule
steps.content:
Rule: the correct evidence must directly support the stated claim; a quotation that addresses a related but different idea does not count as support.
  • Choice A — Vanya says to himself, "I was proud of [Professor Serebrakoff] and of his learning; I received all his words and writings as inspired, and now? Now he has retired, and what is the total of his life? A blank!": Vanya's words express his own disillusionment with the professor's career — not the professor's inability to adjust to the estate. ✗
  • Choice B — Professor Serebrakoff says to Helena, "Ever since I have been old I have been hateful to myself, and I am sure, hateful to you all as well.": the professor expresses self-loathing in general, not specific maladjustment to the estate. ✗
  • Choice C — Professor Serebrakoff says, "I am a book-worm and a scholar, and am unfamiliar with practical affairs. I cannot, I find, dispense with the help of well-informed people such as you, Ivan, and you, Telegin, and you, mother.": the professor acknowledges unfamiliarity with practical matters, but says nothing about being unable to accept estate life. ✗
  • Choice D — Professor Serebrakoff says to Helena, "I am used to my library and the lecture hall and to the esteem and admiration of my colleagues. Now I suddenly find myself plunged in this wilderness [the estate], condemned to see the same stupid people from morning till night and listen to their futile conversation.": the professor directly contrasts his former comfortable life with being "condemned" to the estate, explicitly expressing his inability to accept country life. ✓
D is correct.
